title: No Jokes This Year: A Mentalist Will Try to Read Trump's Mind at the White House Dinner
summary:
For nearly a century, the White House Correspondents' Association Dinner has followed a pretty predictable formula: politicians, journalists, and a comedian who roasts everyone in the room. But this year, organizers decided to shake things up. Instead of a comedian, the headliner is a mentalist — a performer who makes it look like they can read minds.

His name is Oz Pearlman, and he's 43 years old. You might recognize him from social media clips where he guesses strangers' PIN numbers or predicts what they're thinking. He finished third on America's Got Talent in 2015, and since then, he's performed for Fortune 500 CEOs, professional athletes, and late-night TV hosts.

But this gig is different. President Trump plans to attend — his first time at the dinner as president since 2011. And Pearlman hints that Trump won't just be watching; he'll be part of the act.

"Reading Donald Trump's mind is arguably the most impressive thing you could ever do," Pearlman told NPR. He says he's been planning this moment for a decade: "I have been formulating what it will be, how it will play, every minutia of it for 10 years."

So how does mentalism actually work? It's a form of magic where instead of pulling a rabbit out of a hat, the performer seems to pluck thoughts from your mind. But don't worry — it's not real mind-reading. According to experts, mentalists use research, suggestion, showmanship, and sometimes even hidden information (like something you wrote down or Googled) to create the illusion.

Anthony Barnhart, a professor of psychological science and former magician, says mentalism can be just as funny as comedy. "Oftentimes, the natural response to experiencing magic or mentalism is laughter," he explains. So the dinner might feel similar to previous years, just with a different kind of entertainment.

Pearlman hopes his performance will bring people together, even in a room full of political disagreements. "I think that for 25 minutes they're going to laugh, they're going to applaud, they're going to have their jaws drop," he says. "I think as a country, we need that at times."

Will he actually stump the president? We'll find out Saturday night. But Pearlman is confident: if his act goes as planned, "it will be the reason you talk about me for years to come."
why_it_matters: This event reflects the shifting boundaries between politics and entertainment, and it tests whether a non-comedic performer can bridge deep partisan divides in a room full of media and government figures.

background_read: The White House Correspondents’ Association Dinner (WHCD) dates back to 1920. It has historically featured a comedian or satirist who roasts the president and the press. Past comedians include Stephen Colbert, Seth Meyers, and Michelle Wolf, whose 2018 jokes sparked controversy.

Mentalism is a branch of magic that focuses on psychological tricks, cold reading, and influence. Famous mentalists include The Amazing Kreskin and Derren Brown. Unlike stage magic, mentalism often claims to read or control minds, though it relies on natural principles.

President Trump has had a tense relationship with the media, often labeling news organizations as 'enemy of the people.' His attendance at the WHCD is notable because he skipped it during most of his first term, attending only in 2011 (when he was not yet president) and 2025.

perspectives.description: Choosing a mentalist breaks the predictable pattern and might attract new viewers. Pearlman’s act could provide a refreshing, unifying experience that emphasizes wonder over political division.

Some journalists may feel that comedy is a vital tradition that allows for political satire. A mentalist might avoid the uncomfortable but necessary critiques that comedians offer, making the dinner less meaningful.
The WHCD has always been about soft power and public relations. This shift shows that organizers are experimenting with format to adapt to a polarized media environment where jokes can backfire.

If Pearlman succeeds, future dinners may include more varied performers. If he fails, the tradition may revert to comedy. Either way, the dinner’s role as a symbol of press-presidential relations continues to evolve.
